# What FlixelGDX is

FlixelGDX is a Java 17 game framework built on libGDX. Its API philosophy closely follows
HaxeFlixel (and classic ActionScript Flixel): states, sprites, groups, cameras, tweens,
timers, and approachable helpers for beginners. It is not officially affiliated with
HaxeFlixel or libGDX.

Primary sources of truth in the repo
- PROJECT.md: Gradle modules and responsibilities.
- COMPILING.md: Developer workflows, IDE setup, TeaVM notes, consumer Gradle wiring.
- CONTRIBUTING.md: Contribution expectations.
- README.md: Quick orientation and JitPack coordinates.
- Published Javadoc (GitHub Pages): API-level detail.

Java and tooling baseline
- Language level: Java 17 (records, modern switch, lambdas where appropriate).
- Build: Gradle (wrapper-driven). Root project aggregates modules; most logic lives in
  scripts under gradle/.
- Group ID for published artifacts in this repo: me.stringdotjar.flixelgdx (JitPack uses
  com.github.flixelgdx.flixel... transforms for consumers; see README).

Relationship to libGDX
- FlixelGDX depends on libGDX for windowing, graphics, input, filesystem abstraction,
  and asset plumbing.
- Game subclasses extend Flixel types that mirror Flixel/HaxeFlixel patterns while still
  drawing through libGDX batches and textures where relevant.

# High-level architecture analogy

Think of libGDX as the engine room (window, OpenGL, files, audio backends). FlixelGDX is
the studio layout built on top: scenes are states, actors are sprites and groups, and the
global Flixel singleton wires managers so gameplay code reads similarly across projects.

Core gameplay vocabulary (names game developers see most)
- FlixelGame: Application listener subclass that owns the main loop split into update(float)
  and draw(Batch). Implements lifecycle hooks aligned with libGDX plus Flixel-specific flow.
- FlixelState: Stackable scene/state object (switch states through Flixel.switchState).
- FlixelSprite / FlixelObject / FlixelBasic: Entity hierarchy with update/draw/destroy.
- FlixelCamera: Camera wrapper integrating scroll, zoom, FX hooks, and viewport behavior.
- Flixel: Static facade for globals (input managers, audio manager, logger, signals,
  runtime mode, debug overlay hooks, etc.). Initialized once via Flixel.initialize(game).
- Groups: FlixelBasicGroup, FlixelSpriteGroup for ordered membership and batch updates.

Lifecycle conventions (critical for correct suggestions)
- Prefer update(float elapsed) for logic and draw(Batch batch) for rendering on gameplay types
  that integrate with FlixelGame routing.
- destroy() tears down native resources and registrations; distinguish kill/revive semantics
  documented on FlixelBasic when pooling or recycling objects.
- FlixelGame.destroy/dispose stops subsystems and signals end-of-game cleanup.

# Static initialization and launch order (desktop LWJGL3 example)

Typical desktop bootstrap inside FlixelLwjgl3Launcher.launch:
1. FlixelRuntimeUtil.setRuntimeProbe(new FlixelJvmRuntimeProbe())
2. Backend bindings: alerter, window, host integration, stack trace provider, reflection,
   log file handler, sound backend factory, runtime mode, optional debug overlay factory.
3. Flixel.initialize(game)
4. Construct Lwjgl3Application(game, configuration)
Inside FlixelGame.create():
- configureCrashHandler runs first.
- When Flixel.canStoreLogs() is true and a log file handler exists, Flixel.startFileLogging()
  runs so JVM file logging begins after Gdx.files is available.

Logging system (JVM file logging and consoles)
- FlixelLogger formats console lines according to FlixelLogMode and optionally mirrors plain,
  detailed lines to FlixelLogFileHandler when active.
- Desktop/Android JVM backends register FlixelJvmLogFileHandler before initialize().
- Web (TeaVM) omits a file handler; Flixel.startFileLogging() remains safe because the handler
  is null.
- Default logs directory resolution uses FlixelRuntimeUtil.RuntimeProbe. FlixelJvmRuntimeProbe
  classifies RunEnvironment as IDE, JAR, or CLASSPATH and computes a .../logs folder:
  - IDE: infer Gradle module roots from java.class.path, walk user.dir upward to cover sibling
    modules, and reject inferred roots that do not line up with user.dir (avoids dropping logs
    under only the framework module, a composite-build checkout, or another unrelated folder).
  - JAR distribution: logs beside the fat jar after validating Main-Class manifest semantics.
- Override with FlixelGame.setLogsFolder(absolutePath) or Flixel.setLogsFolder before create().
- Rotation: FlixelLogger supports max log files; handler prunes oldest timestamped files.

Gradle bytecode logging plugin (optional consumer tooling)
- Module: flixelgdx-logging-plugin.
- Rewrites FlixelLogger info/warn/error calls to *WithSite variants and static Flixel.info/warn/error calls to
  bytecode hooks so file and line come from bytecode constants instead of stack walking (TeaVM).
- Consumers apply me.stringdotjar.flixelgdx.logging on modules where logs originate.

Module map (what each published-ish piece is for)
- flixelgdx-core: Framework heart (entities, states, cameras, tweens, timers, input action sets,
  saves, debug overlay plumbing, asset helpers). Exports me.stringdotjar.flixelgdx.logging API.
- flixelgdx-common: Shared JVM-friendly helpers consumed by multiple backends (MiniAudio-based
  sound handler wiring shared across desktop/Android/iOS launch paths).
- flixelgdx-jvm: JVM-only utilities not suitable for TeaVM (stack traces, FlixelJvmLogFileHandler,
  FlixelJvmRuntimeProbe).
- flixelgdx-lwjgl3: Desktop LWJGL3 launcher glue (window listener chaining, debug overlays using
  ImGui optional paths, mouse icons, host integrations).
- flixelgdx-android: Android launcher integration with libGDX AndroidApplication lifecycle.
- flixelgdx-ios: iOS bootstrap targeting MobiVM/RoboVM-style workflows described in COMPILING.md.
- flixelgdx-teavm: TeaVM web backend (console sinks instead of ANSI terminals; filesystem limits).
- flixelgdx-teavm-plugin: Gradle automation around TeaVM builds for games targeting browsers.
- flixelgdx-logging-plugin: bytecode weaving described above.
- flixelgdx-test: Internal JUnit harness for framework tests (not shipped as game dependency).

Runtime classification helpers
- FlixelRuntimeMode (RELEASE/DEBUG/TEST-style distinctions used by launchers).
- FlixelRuntimeUtil.RunEnvironment from JVM probe separates IDE vs packaged jar vs generic
  classpath for paths and diagnostics.

Performance and memory rules assistants must respect when editing framework code
- Never allocate per frame inside hot paths unless unavoidable; reuse buffers and pooled types.
- Prefer Flixel/libGDX collections (Array, ObjectMap) where the codebase already does.
- Follow project EditorConfig (brace rules differ slightly for empty methods).
- No star imports; import concrete types explicitly.
- Avoid needless widening refactors: align changes with existing naming and documentation tone.

Signals and global hooks
- Flixel.Signals exposes coarse lifecycle broadcasts (pre/post update/draw, window focus, etc.).
  Prefer these when extending framework behavior without subclassing FlixelGame everywhere.

Asset and animation highlights (for context when reading issues)
- Bitmap fonts and registry helpers live alongside libGDX asset handles.
- Adobe Animate-style rigs use FlixelAnimateRigLoader output consumed by FlixelAnimateSprite;
  baked affine transforms aim to keep draw paths lightweight.

Testing expectations for contributors and CI-minded assistants
- Framework functionality is validated via flixelgdx-test and manual integration in separate
  consumer libGDX projects using Maven Local, composite builds, or JitPack snapshots.
- TeaVM builds require reflection profiles declared via gradle.properties keys documented in
  COMPILING.md.

Common pitfalls for AI-generated patches
- Calling Flixel.initialize() twice or registering backends after initialization (methods guard
  against this with IllegalStateException).
- Assuming java.class.path layout identical across Gradle, IntelliJ, Eclipse, or composite
  includes when deriving filesystem roots (always route path discovery through FlixelRuntimeUtil).
- Using desktop-only APIs on TeaVM (process spawning, arbitrary filesystem writes).
- Adding heavy reflection on paths executed every frame.

Documentation tone when editing Javadoc or comments in-repo
- Use beginner-friendly language with concrete examples on heavily used APIs.
- Prefer ASCII punctuation in narrative comments; keep richer formatting structured inside
  Javadoc HTML tags where required.

Where to start reading source code
- Launch glue: flixelgdx-lwjgl3/.../FlixelLwjgl3Launcher.java
- Global facade: flixelgdx-core/.../Flixel.java
- Game loop host: flixelgdx-core/.../FlixelGame.java
- Logging: flixelgdx-core/.../logging plus flixelgdx-jvm/.../logging
- Runtime probe: flixelgdx-jvm/.../FlixelJvmRuntimeProbe.java

Contact points for dependency coordinates
- internal Gradle publications use me.stringdotjar.flixelgdx artifacts.
- JitPack coordinates mirror GitHub organization naming; verify README before advising consumers.
